prompt: I need to pay Machine Games Duty, but I'm not sure how to calculate the amount I owe. What steps should I take to ensure I file the correct return and make the payment on time?
response: To calculate your Machine Games Duty, you need to determine your total turnover from gaming machines during your accounting period. This is usually 3 months unless you've agreed with HMRC for a different period. Once you've calculated your turnover, you'll need to file your return and pay the duty within 30 days of the end of your accounting period. If the deadline falls on a weekend or bank holiday, you must file and pay by the last working day before it, unless you're using Faster Payments, debit, or credit card online. Make sure you choose a payment method that allows you to meet the deadline, as late payments may result in penalties and interest.
stratum: Money and tax | procedural | medium | UK-wide | False | False
generated_response:
Below is the quickest way to work out your Machine Games Duty (MGD), file the return and pay on time.

1. Check you’re registered  
   • You must be registered for MGD before you can file a return. If you have not already done so, register online through Government Gateway (it takes about 15 minutes and you must register at least 14 days before your machines are made available for play).  

2. Gather the figures for the accounting period  
   • An MGD accounting period is normally three months (ending 31 March, 30 June, 30 September and 31 December).  
   • For each machine, note:  
     – Total stakes/coins inserted  
     – Total cash prizes paid out  
     – Refunds, cancelled plays and VAT (these should be excluded).  
   • Net takings = stakes received minus cash prizes paid out.

3. Identify the duty rate for each machine  
   • Lower rate (5%) – maximum stake 10p, maximum cash prize £8.  
   • Standard rate (20%) – chargeable proportion up to and including 20%.  
   • Higher rate (25%) – chargeable proportion over 20%.  
   (Most fruit-machines and quiz machines fall into the standard rate; higher-jackpot machines usually fall into the higher rate.)

4. Calculate the duty  
   • For each machine: Net takings × applicable rate.  
   • Add the duty from all machines to get the amount due for the period.

5. Complete the MGD return  
   • Log in to your MGD account on GOV.UK.  
   • Enter total net takings and duty due (the online form does the final calculation check for you).  
   • Submit the return no later than 30 days after the end of the period (for example, period ending 30 June – return due 30 July).

6. Pay the duty by the same deadline  
   • Faster Payments, CHAPS or debit card – usually same-day/next working day.  
   • Bacs – allow 3 working days.  
   • Direct Debit – set up at least 10 working days before the first collection; HMRC collects on the due date.  
   • Quote your 15-digit MGD reference with every payment.

7. Keep records  
   • Keep all MGD calculations, machine takings and payout records for at least 4 years in case HMRC asks to see them.

If anything is unclear, or your machines don’t fit the usual stake/prize limits, phone the HMRC MGD helpline on 0300 200 3700 before you submit.

Machine Games Duty – rates, registration, returns and payment: https://www.gov.uk/guidance/machine-games-duty

prompt: I heard about the 'Breathing Space' scheme. Can I still proceed with a bankruptcy petition if the debtor is using this scheme?
response: No, you cannot present a bankruptcy petition if the debtor is currently protected under the 'Breathing Space' scheme. This scheme offers temporary relief from creditors, allowing the debtor to negotiate repayment plans without facing legal action. If the debtor is using this scheme, you should explore alternative methods to recover the money, such as mediation or contacting the Insolvency Service for guidance. It's important to check the status of the debtor's financial situation before proceeding with any formal legal actions.
stratum: Money and tax | procedural | medium | UK-wide | False | False
generated_response:
No. Once you receive notification that a debtor has entered a Breathing Space (Debt Respite) moratorium you must not present a new bankruptcy petition against them.  

For the whole of the moratorium:  
1. Pause any enforcement action, including starting bankruptcy proceedings.  
2. Stop adding interest, fees or charges to the qualifying debt.  
3. If you had already presented a petition before the Breathing Space started, the court will adjourn the hearing until the moratorium ends. You must not take any further steps unless the court gives specific permission.  
4. Keep an eye on the end-date in the Insolvency Service’s Breathing Space register or the notification you were sent.  
   • Standard Breathing Space lasts 60 days.  
   • A Mental Health Crisis Breathing Space lasts for the period of the crisis treatment plus 30 days, so it can be much longer.  
5. As soon as the moratorium ends you are free to reassess the situation and, if you still wish, file a bankruptcy petition in the usual way.

Trying to continue or start bankruptcy during Breathing Space could lead the court to dismiss your petition and you may be ordered to pay costs, so it is safest to wait.

• Regulations bar creditors from “giving notice or petitioning for bankruptcy” during a Breathing Space.  
• Enforcement pauses protect debtors and are mandatory once you receive official notice.  
• Court practice notes confirm petitions already issued are adjourned, not proceeded with.  
• Breaching the moratorium can expose a creditor to costs or sanctions, so waiting is the low-risk route.

prompt: I'm a teacher in Scotland and I need to apply for a National Insurance number. What do I need to do and how long will it take?

prompt: I work as a nurse and need to understand if I have to register with HMRC for anti-money laundering regulations. What are the requirements and steps to register?
response: If you are a nurse and your work involves handling money or financial transactions, you might need to register with HMRC for anti-money laundering regulations. The regulations apply to certain professions like accountants, tax advisers, and others. You should check if your role falls under the list of professions that require registration. If you need to register, you should contact the relevant supervisory authority for your profession. For example, if you are an accountant, you might need to register with the Association of Chartered Certified Accountants (ACCA). You can find more information on the HMRC website.
stratum: Money and tax | procedural | medium | UK-wide | False | False
generated_response:
You do not need to register with HMRC for anti-money-laundering supervision if you are working solely as a nurse (whether employed by the NHS, an agency, or self-employed). HMRC’s money-laundering regulations apply to businesses that carry on certain high-risk financial or professional activities – for example:  

1. Accountancy service providers  
2. Tax advisers  
3. Estate and letting agents  
4. High-value dealers, art market participants, casinos, money-service businesses, trust or company service providers, etc.  

Healthcare professionals, including nurses, are not on that list. Unless you also run one of the activities above as a separate business, you have no obligation to register, pay the annual supervision fee, or carry out the related customer-due-diligence checks.

If you ever expand into a line of work that does fall under the regulations (for example, setting up a bookkeeping side business), you would then need to:

1. Check whether a professional body already supervises you – if not, HMRC will be the default supervisor.  
2. Register online through your Government Gateway account before you start trading in that activity.  
3. Pay the registration and annual renewal fees.  
4. Put the required anti-money-laundering policies and staff training in place.  

For full details see “Anti-money-laundering supervision: register or renew” on GOV.UK:  
https://www.gov.uk/guidance/anti-money-laundering-supervision-register-or-renew

prompt: I'm a nurse and I've received a Simple Assessment letter. How do I pay the tax I owe and what happens if I don't pay on time?
response: If you receive a Simple Assessment letter, you must pay the tax you owe by the deadline stated in the letter. You can pay online, by phone, or by post using a payment method that suits you. If you can't pay on time, you should contact HMRC to explain your situation. They may offer you options like spreading the payment over several months or setting up a payment plan. It's important to act quickly to avoid penalties or further action from HMRC.

   • Your payment reference (usually 14 or 15 characters, for example ‘SA123456789A’).  
   • The payment deadline – often 31 January but follow the exact date on the letter.

2. Choose a payment method  
   a) Online or telephone banking (Faster Payments, CHAPS or Bacs)  
      • Use HMRC’s bank account:  
        Account name: HMRC Cumbernauld  
        Sort code: 08-32-10  
        Account number: 12001039  
      • Quote your payment reference.  
      • Same-day with Faster Payments or CHAPS; three working days with Bacs.  

   b) Debit or corporate credit card online  
      • Go to GOV.UK → ‘Pay your Self Assessment tax bill’.  
      • Enter the amount and your payment reference.  
      • Personal credit cards are not accepted.  

   c) Cheque by post  
      • Make it payable to ‘HM Revenue and Customs only’ followed by your payment reference.  
      • Include the payslip from the letter.  
      • Allow at least 3 working days before the deadline.

prompt: I recently moved house and need to update my address with HMRC. How do I do this, and what do I need to prepare?

You'll need to provide your current address and confirm your identity, which usually involves showing photo ID like a passport or driving licence. It's important to wait until you've moved before updating your address with HMRC. This ensures that your new address is correctly recorded and that you receive any relevant communications.

prompt: I'm running a limited company in Wales and need to file my accounts and tax return. What steps should I follow to ensure everything is done correctly and on time?
response: To file your accounts and tax return, you need to use the online service provided by HMRC and Companies House. Ensure your accounts are balanced, meaning your total assets match your liabilities. You'll need your Government Gateway user ID and password, as well as your Companies House password and authentication code if filing together. The service closes on 31 March 2026, so prepare early. If you cannot file online, you can use the paper form CT600 with a reasonable excuse or to file in Welsh, but you must also complete and submit form WT1.

Below is the standard timetable and checklist for a small private limited company based in Wales.  
(HMRC and Companies House rules are UK-wide, so the Welsh location makes no difference to the process.)

1. Confirm your accounting period  
   • Your financial (accounting) year usually ends on the last day of the month you incorporated.  
   • Check the “next accounts made up to” date on the Companies House register.

2. Keep your bookkeeping up to date  
   • Keep invoices, receipts, bank statements and payroll records for at least 6 years.  
   • Make sure they reconcile to your business bank account.

3. Prepare the annual accounts  
   • Profit-and-loss statement, balance sheet, notes and (if not a micro-entity) a directors’ report.  
   • Most small Welsh companies can file “micro-entity” or “abridged” accounts to reduce detail.  
   • Double-check that the balance sheet is signed by a director.

4. File the accounts with Companies House  
   • File online via WebFiling or accounting software using your company authentication code.  
   • Deadline: 9 months after the accounting year-end (21 months after incorporation for your first set).  
   • Late filing penalties start at £150 and rise quickly, so allow a safety margin.

5. Work out the Corporation Tax bill  
   • Adjust your accounting profit for tax rules (capital allowances, disallowed expenses, etc.).  
   • Small companies usually pay 19% or 25% depending on profits; check the current rate bands.

6. Pay Corporation Tax  
   • Payment is due 9 months and 1 day after the accounting period ends.  
   • Pay electronically using the 17-character Corporation Tax reference in your HMRC online account.

7. Submit the Company Tax Return (CT600) to HMRC  
   • Includes the CT600 form, full iXBRL-tagged accounts and detailed tax computations.  
   • Must be filed online through HMRC software or commercial software.  
   • Deadline: 12 months after the accounting period ends (penalties from £100 upwards if late).

8. File the annual Confirmation Statement (CS01)  
   • Confirms shareholdings, officers and PSCs.  
   • Due every 12 months; most companies submit this at the same time each year.  
   • Fee: £13 online.

9. Optional but sensible  
   • Appoint a qualified accountant if you are unsure about tax adjustments or iXBRL tagging.  
   • Set calendar reminders a month before each deadline.  
   • Use Companies House and HMRC email alerts for extra prompts.  
   • If you prefer, you can complete filings through the medium of Welsh by contacting Companies House and HMRC.

10. If something may be late  
   • Contact Companies House or HMRC before the deadline and explain; extensions are rare but possible in exceptional circumstances.  
   • Otherwise, file as soon as possible to limit penalties and interest.

Key GOV.UK pages  
• File annual accounts: https://www.gov.uk/file-annual-accounts  
• Company Tax Returns: https://www.gov.uk/company-tax-returns  
• Corporation Tax: https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax/corporation-tax

prompt: I inherited a pension pot from my late partner and want to know if I need to pay tax on the money I receive. What steps should I take to understand my tax obligations and how to report them to HMRC?
response: Yes, you may need to pay tax on the money you receive from an inherited pension pot. The tax depends on factors like the type of payment, the pension pot type, and the age of the person who died. If the pension pot owner was under 75 when they died, you might have to pay tax if the lump sum is paid more than 2 years after the death or exceeds the lump sum and death benefit allowance. Contact the pension provider to confirm if the payment was discretionary, as this affects Inheritance Tax. You should inform HMRC within 13 months of the death or 30 days after realizing tax is owed. Keep records of all communications and payments for reference.
stratum: Money and tax | procedural | medium | UK-wide | False | False
generated_response:
What you pay depends on three things:
1. The type of payment you are getting.  
   • Regular survivor’s pension from a defined-benefit scheme – the income is treated like wages and is taxed under PAYE.  
   • Lump-sum death benefit or inherited drawdown/annuity from a defined-contribution pot – the tax position depends on your late partner’s age when they died and when the provider pays you.

2. The age of your partner when they died.  
   • Died before age 75 and the pension is paid out within 2 years – normally tax-free.  
   • Died at 75 or older, or the provider pays out after 2 years – the money is taxable at your normal Income Tax rate.

3. How the provider pays you.  
   • Lump sums are often paid with emergency tax deducted.  
   • Regular payments are put on PAYE, with tax taken off each month.

Steps to work out and report any tax:

1. Contact the pension provider  
   • Ask whether the payment is a lump sum, survivor’s pension or flexi-access drawdown.  
   • Ask for written confirmation of:  
     – your late partner’s age at death,  
     – the date the provider first knew of the death,  
     – the gross amount being paid and any tax already deducted,  
     – the P45 or payslip for the payment, if available.

2. Check whether tax should have been taken  
   • Use the information above with the rules shown in points 1-3 to see if the payment ought to be tax-free or taxable.  
   • If it should be tax-free but tax was deducted, you can reclaim it (see step 5).

3. Tell HMRC if you already fill in a Self Assessment tax return  
   • Enter the gross amount of any taxable payments in the ‘UK pension income’ box.  
   • Include any tax already deducted so HMRC can work out any balance due or refund.

4. If you do not normally complete Self Assessment  
   • HMRC will usually adjust your tax code once they receive the P45 details from the pension payer.  
   • If you think the code is wrong, phone HMRC on 0300 200 3300 (have your National Insurance number and the provider’s letter to hand).

5. Reclaim overpaid tax on a lump-sum death benefit (if tax was taken but the payment should be tax-free)  
   • Download form R40 and send it to HMRC; or  
   • Claim online through your Personal Tax Account.

6. Keep all paperwork for at least 22 months after the end of the tax year, in case HMRC asks for evidence.

Useful GOV.UK guidance  
• Paying tax on your private pension: https://www.gov.uk/tax-on-pension  
• Self Assessment: https://www.gov.uk/self-assessment-tax-returns  
• Claim a tax refund form R40: https://www.gov.uk/claim-tax-refund

If at any point you are unsure, call HMRC or consider speaking to a regulated financial adviser.

• Assumes user received an inherited UK pension, the key factors are payment type and age at death under UK tax rules.  
• Provides step-by-step process suitable for low digital literacy: contact provider, check treatment, report or reclaim.  
• Includes the main GOV.UK links that explain pension tax and Self Assessment.  
• Shows both PAYE and Self Assessment routes, covering most common situations.

prompt: What should I do if I'm facing a court claim for debts and I'm not sure whether to go bankrupt or seek other options?
response: If you're facing a court claim for debts, you should consider contacting an official receiver who manages the first stage of bankruptcies and insolvency cases. They collect and protect assets for creditors, investigate the reasons for insolvency, and act as a trustee or liquidator when no private sector insolvency practitioner is appointed. However, they do not provide debt advice, so you should seek free debt advice from Citizens Advice or National Debtline instead. It's important to understand your options and the potential consequences of bankruptcy before making a decision.
stratum: Money and tax | procedural | medium | UK-wide | False | False
generated_response:
If you receive a claim form from the county court (often called a ‘CCJ claim’) you need to act quickly – even if you think bankruptcy might be the end result.  

1. Check the claim form date. You normally have 14 days from the date it was served to send back the Acknowledgement of Service or a full reply (the N9A/N9B forms).  
2. Gather the facts:  
   • the exact amount owed,  
   • whether you agree you owe it, and  
   • any evidence (statements, correspondence) that supports your position.  
3. Get free, independent debt advice before you decide on bankruptcy or another solution. Contact one of these services immediately:  
   • National Debtline – 0808 808 4000 (England).  
   • StepChange Debt Charity – 0800 138 1111 or stepchange.org.  
   • Citizens Advice – citizensadvice.org.uk or your local office.  
4. While you wait for advice, complete and return the court forms so you don’t get a default judgment. You can:  
   • admit the debt and offer affordable instalments,  
   • dispute the debt (give reasons and any evidence), or  
   • ask for 28 days extra by sending an Acknowledgement of Service.  
5. Discuss all debt-relief options with the adviser:  
   • Debt Management Plan – flexible, informal, keeps assets.  
   • Individual Voluntary Arrangement (IVA) – formal agreement, lasts 5-6 years, avoids bankruptcy.  
   • Debt Relief Order (DRO) – for debts under £30,000, low assets/income.  
   • Bankruptcy – writes off most debts after 12 months but you may lose assets and it affects credit for 6 years.  
6. If bankruptcy is the best route, your adviser will help you apply online (£680 fee). If another solution suits, they’ll help you set that up and inform the court/creditor.  
7. Keep copies of everything you send and make sure the court deadlines are met. Missing a deadline can result in the court entering judgment against you, adding costs and enforcement action.

Key GOV.UK pages:  
• Bankruptcy – how to apply, what happens: https://www.gov.uk/bankruptcy  
• Individual Voluntary Arrangements: https://www.gov.uk/individual-voluntary-arrangements  
• Replying to a county court claim: https://www.gov.uk/respond-to-court-claim-for-money

• Included relevant GOV.UK links for full, authoritative detail.  
• Steps are in plain English, short paragraphs, and numbered for easy following.

prompt: I'm a retired person who has been paying the reduced rate of National Insurance. How do I stop paying the reduced rate and start paying the standard rate?
response: To stop paying the reduced rate and start paying the standard rate, you need to inform HMRC that your circumstances have changed. You can do this by filling in form CF9 or CF9A (if you're a widow) and sending it to HMRC. Your employer will complete part 2 of your certificate of election and return it to you. You should also tell your employer about this change. If you're self-employed, you should register to pay Class 2 National Insurance immediately. Once you give up your right to the reduced rate, you cannot claim it again.
stratum: Money and tax | procedural | medium | UK-wide | False | False
generated_response:
Answer:
Only a few very specific groups can pay a “reduced rate” of National Insurance.  
The two most common are:  
• the Married Woman’s or Widow’s reduced-rate election (only ever available to women who opted in before 11 May 1977), and  
• self-employed people who claimed the old Small Earnings Exception (now replaced by the Small Profits Threshold).

If you are a man aged 65 + it is unlikely you are legally paying a reduced rate at all, because:

1. Once you reach State Pension age you normally stop paying National Insurance on any earnings.  
2. The Married Woman’s/Widow’s election has never applied to men.

What to do next

1. Check your most recent payslip or the letter from HMRC that shows the type of National Insurance you are paying (Class 1 letter, Class 2, Class 3, etc.).  
2. If you think the wrong rate is being taken, phone HMRC’s National Insurance helpline on 0300 200 3500 (textphone 0300 200 3519). Have your National Insurance number to hand.  
3. Explain that you believe you are paying a reduced rate and want to confirm whether you should be paying National Insurance at all, and—if so—at the standard rate.  
4. HMRC will:  
   a. confirm whether you are within a group entitled to reduced-rate NI,  
   b. tell you if any form (for example CF9) is needed to cancel the reduced-rate election, or  
   c. correct your record and instruct your employer’s payroll (or tell you how to pay the correct voluntary Class 3 rate) if an error has been made.  
5. Keep any written confirmation from HMRC. If you are still working, give a copy to your employer’s payroll so they deduct the correct rate in future.

Relevant GOV.UK link  
National Insurance: general guidance – https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax

Justification:
• A male pensioner is normally exempt from NI, so paying any rate—reduced or standard—may be an error.  
• Only HMRC can confirm the contributor’s category and, if necessary, issue or cancel a reduced-rate election.  
• The steps focus on checking records first, then contacting HMRC, which is the safest way to correct NI status.  
• GOV.UK helpline and forms are the official route; linking to the general NI page keeps advice up to date.

Missing info: Are you still receiving employment or self-employment earnings, or are you making voluntary contributions only?

response: If your relative is in care and needs help with their tax credits, you should contact HMRC to discuss the options available. As an appointee, you can take responsibility for their claim if they cannot manage it themselves. It's important to understand the difference between these roles and choose the one that best suits your situation. You should also ensure you have the proper documentation and authorization before taking any action on their behalf.

If your relative is no longer able to manage their own affairs, you can look after their tax-credit claim by following these steps:

1. Get legal authority to act for them  
   • If they already made a Lasting Power of Attorney (LPA) for property and financial affairs, register it with HMRC.  
   • If there is no LPA and they cannot now make one, apply to become their “appointee” for tax credits through the Tax Credit Office. Phone 0345 300 3900 and ask for form TC689 (“Authority to act”). HMRC will arrange a short interview before confirming the appointeeship.

2. Collect the details you will need  
   • Their National Insurance number, tax-credit award notice, recent payslips or benefit letters, details of any childcare payments, and the date they moved into care.

3. Tell HMRC about every relevant change within 1 month  
   • Date they entered the care home or hospital.

prompt: I'm a student in Wales and I want to know what information I need to provide to use the Self Assessment tax calculator for estimating my tax bill. What details should I enter?
response: To use the Self Assessment tax calculator, you need to provide details of your income sources. This includes profits from self-employment, rental income from property, income from a State Pension or occupational pension, and income from paid employment. The calculator will use this information to estimate your Income Tax and Class 4 National Insurance. It will not consider the High Income Child Benefit Charge, savings, investments, or other payments like tax from employment. Make sure you have accurate figures for each income type to get a reliable estimate.

1. Personal details  
   • National Insurance number

   • Whether you were resident in the UK for the whole tax year  

2. Income you received in the tax year  
   a. Employment  
      – Total pay and tax already deducted (P60 or last P45)  
   b. Self-employment or casual work  
      – Turnover and allowable expenses to give your profit or loss  
   c. Savings and investments  
      – Interest from bank or building-society accounts (gross and tax deducted)  
      – Dividends from UK or overseas shares  
   d. Property (if any)  
      – Rental income and related expenses  
   e. Other taxable income  
      – Scholarships or bursaries that are taxable  
      – Taxable state benefits (e.g. Jobseeker’s Allowance)  
      – Capital gains if you sold shares or other assets  

   Note: Maintenance loans and most student grants are not taxable, so you do not enter them.

3. Tax already paid or deducted at source  
   • PAYE tax from P60/P45  
   • Tax deducted by banks on interest (if any)  
   • Any payments on account you have already made for Self Assessment  

4. Deductions, reliefs and allowances you want to claim

   • Student loan repayments already taken through PAYE (Plan 1, Plan 2 or Post-graduate loan)  
   • Professional subscriptions or course-related expenses you have paid yourself  
   • Marriage Allowance transfer or Blind Person’s Allowance if these apply  

5. Other information  
   • Class 2 National Insurance (if you were self-employed)  
   • Any losses you want to carry forward or sideways  
   • Whether you want to claim Child Benefit High-Income Charge or repay coronavirus support grants  

Enter each figure in the calculator exactly as it appears on your paperwork; the tool will do the rest and show how much tax you still owe or are due back.

Most students only need their P60 (or payslips), any bank-interest statement and details of pension or Gift Aid payments, but it is worth checking every heading above to be sure nothing is missed.

prompt: I need to submit my Self Assessment tax return, but I'm not sure if I should do it online or print it out. What's the best way to go about it?

It's important to ensure you have all your financial records ready, such as bank statements and receipts, to accurately complete the form. Online filing is recommended as it reduces the risk of errors and allows for quicker processing. If you're unsure about the process, you can seek help from HMRC or a professional advisor.

For most people the quickest, easiest and safest way to file a Self Assessment return is online through GOV.​UK.

Advantages of filing online  
• You get an extra three months to file – deadline is 31 January (paper returns must reach HMRC by 31 October).  
• HMRC work out your tax as you go, so you can see what you owe before you submit.  
• Any refund is processed faster.  
• You receive immediate confirmation that HMRC has your return.  
• You can amend the return online up to 12 months after the 31 January deadline.

How to file online  
1. Check you have a Government Gateway user ID and password.  
   • If you’ve never filed online, select ‘Create sign in details’ and follow the prompts.  
   • HMRC will post you an activation code – allow up to 10 days (21 days if abroad).  
2. Sign in at gov.​uk and add the Self Assessment service to your account.  
3. Gather the information you’ll need (for example P60s, P45s, invoices, bank interest, pension contributions, Gift Aid donations).  
4. Complete each section of the online return. Use the ‘Save and continue later’ button if you need breaks.  
5. Check the calculation HMRC shows, correct any errors, then select ‘Submit’.  
6. Make any payment due by 31 January (and 31 July if you have a second payment on account).  
7. Download or print the receipt page for your records.

If printing/paper might suit you  
Use form SA100 and any relevant supplementary pages. They must reach HMRC by 31 October. Allow time for postage and keep proof of posting. Note that paper returns are processed manually, so refunds and any HMRC queries can take longer.

Help and guidance  
GOV.​UK Self Assessment: https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax  
HMRC Self Assessment helpline: 0300 200 3310 (Mon–Fri)

prompt: If I am a landlord with multiple flats rented out on assured shorthold tenancies, how many votes do I get in an RTM company and what happens if the RTM process doesn't result in the building being managed by them?
response: As a landlord with multiple flats rented out on assured shorthold tenancies, you get one vote for each flat you own. This means if you own four flats, you get four votes. The RTM company must still pay for any costs you incur during the management transfer process, even if the RTM process does not result in the building being managed by them. This includes any legal or administrative fees related to setting up the RTM company. It's important to understand your voting rights and the financial implications of the RTM process before proceeding.
stratum: Housing and local services | procedural | medium | UK-wide | False | False
generated_response:
In a Right to Manage (RTM) company you get one vote for each flat you own on a long lease.  
• At a show-of-hands vote you still only raise one hand, but if any member asks for a poll (a count based on flats) you are credited with one vote per qualifying flat – so three flats means three votes.  

If the RTM claim is not completed (for example the company withdraws, misses a deadline or the tribunal refuses the claim):  
1. Management stays exactly where it is now – with the freeholder or the managing agent they appoint.  
2. The leaseholders who signed up to the RTM still have to pay the landlord’s ‘reasonable costs’ incurred in dealing with the claim.  
3. The same group can start a fresh RTM application, but they must wait 12 months and serve a new claim notice.  
4. If leaseholders still want change sooner, they can instead:  
   a) apply to the First-tier Tribunal for the appointment of an independent manager, or  
   b) explore collective enfranchisement (buying the freehold).  

More detail is on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/right-to-manage-a-landlord
extracted_justification:
• RTM model articles (The RTM Companies (Model Articles) Regulations 2009) give each qualifying tenant one vote per flat on a poll; a show of hands counts members, not flats.  
• Landlords who sub-let on assured shorthold tenancies are still the qualifying tenants for RTM purposes, so the votes attach to the flats they hold.  
• If the RTM claim fails, the statutory position is that control reverts to the freeholder and costs are payable (Commonhold and Leasehold Reform Act 2002, s.99–100).  
• The 12-month bar and the alternative routes (appointed manager or buying the freehold) are the main legally recognised next steps.

prompt: My leaseholder association is not recognized by the landlord. How can I resolve this issue through the tribunal?
response: If your leaseholder association is not recognized by the landlord, you can apply to the First-Tier Tribunal to address this dispute. The tribunal will review the situation and determine if the association should be recognized. You'll need to provide evidence such as the association's formation documents and any communications with the landlord. The process is free and the tribunal is independent of government. It's important to gather all relevant information to support your case.
stratum: Housing and local services | procedural | medium | UK-wide | False | False
generated_response:
If your landlord refuses to recognise your leaseholders’ association, you can ask the First-tier Tribunal (Property Chamber) to issue a “certificate of recognition”. Once you have the certificate, the landlord must treat your group as a Recognised Tenants’ Association (RTA).

Follow these steps.

1. Check you meet the basic requirements  
   • At least 50% of the qualifying leaseholders in the block must be members (the tribunal may accept slightly less in small blocks).  
   • The association needs simple written rules (a constitution) that cover: name and address, aim of representing members on service-charge and management issues, how people join, voting rights, and how officers are elected.  
   • The association must be independent of the landlord and managing agent.

2. Gather evidence  
   • A membership list with flat numbers and signatures or written confirmation from each member.  
   • A copy of your constitution.  
   • Minutes of recent meetings.  
   • Copies of any letters/emails where you have already asked the landlord to recognise the association and their reply (or lack of reply).

3. Complete the tribunal application  
   • Use the form for “Leasehold – Other” applications (ask the tribunal office if you are unsure which to use).  
   • Quote section 29 of the Landlord and Tenant Act 1985 and state you are applying for a certificate of recognition.  
   • Attach the evidence listed above.  
   • Pay the £100 application fee (a further £200 hearing fee is payable only if a hearing is needed).  
   • Send the form and documents to the regional First-tier Tribunal (Property Chamber – Residential Property) that covers your area, and send a copy to the landlord.

4. After you apply  
   • The tribunal may ask for extra information or arrange an informal hearing where both sides can speak.  
   • If satisfied, the tribunal issues the certificate (usually valid for 4–5 years).  
   • Give a copy to the landlord and keep it safe—renew it before it expires.

5. If the landlord still ignores the association  
   • Show them the certificate—by law they must now consult the RTA on major works and give financial summaries.  
   • If they fail to do so, you can take further action through the tribunal or county court.

Useful link  
Find the application form, fees and your local tribunal office under “Leasehold and shared ownership problems” on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services
extracted_justification:
• Assumes the user wants formal recognition (certificate) so the landlord must engage.  
• Steps follow Landlord and Tenant Act 1985, section 29, and First-tier Tribunal process.  
• Covers eligibility, evidence, forms, fees and post-decision rights in plain English.  
• Provides GOV.UK path for official forms and guidance.  
• No critical information is missing for the user to start the application.

prompt: Is there any support available for people who have missed mortgage payments?
response: Support for Mortgage Interest (SMI) cannot be used to cover missed mortgage payments. However, you should consider speaking to your lender about options such as payment holidays or interest-only payments. These alternatives might help you manage your mortgage arrears. It's important to address missed payments promptly to avoid further complications.
stratum: Housing and local services | procedural | medium | UK-wide | False | False
generated_response:
Help is available. Act quickly—support options close off if you fall further behind.

1. Speak to your lender  
   • Tell them you’re struggling. By UK rules they must consider options such as a temporary payment plan, switching to interest-only or extending the term.  
   • If your lender has signed the “Mortgage Charter”, you can normally ask for:  
     – Up to 6 months on interest-only, or  
     – A term extension you can reverse within 6 months, and  
     – No repossession for 12 months after your first missed payment.

2. Get free, independent debt advice  
   • Contact Citizens Advice Scotland (0800 028 1456), StepChange (0800 138 1111) or MoneyHelper (0800 138 7777).  
   • An adviser can speak to the lender for you, check benefits and outline Scottish schemes.

3. Check benefit support and government loans  
   • If you receive Universal Credit, Pension Credit or certain legacy benefits, you may qualify for a Support for Mortgage Interest (SMI) loan to cover interest on up to £200,000 of your mortgage.  
   • You can ask Jobcentre Plus or the Universal Credit helpline to add SMI—there is no asset test.

4. Consider “Breathing Space”  
   • A registered debt adviser can apply for Breathing Space. For 60 days, most interest, fees and court action are frozen while you work on a plan.

5. Scottish Home Owners’ Support Fund  
   • If you are at risk of repossession, you may apply for:  
     – Mortgage to Rent: a social landlord buys your home and rents it back to you.  
     – Mortgage to Shared Equity: the Scottish Government buys a share, reducing what you owe.  
   • Apply through a debt adviser or download forms from mygov.scot.

6. Check for insurance or workplace help  
   • Mortgage payment protection insurance, critical-illness cover or a teacher union hardship fund may pay your arrears.

7. Keep records and get everything in writing  
   • Note dates, who you spoke to and what was agreed.  
   • Send follow-up emails or letters confirming any repayment plan.

Useful links  
Housing and local services (GOV.UK): https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services  
MoneyHelper mortgage arrears guide (search “MoneyHelper missed mortgage payments”).  
Scottish Home Owners’ Support Fund (search “mygov.scot home owners support fund”).
extracted_justification:
• Early contact with the lender is required under FCA mortgage conduct rules and the Mortgage Charter gives extra short-term options.  
• Free, regulated debt advice improves outcomes; services listed have Scottish coverage.  
• SMI is the main UK-wide government help once benefits are in payment.  
• Breathing Space offers legal protection while a plan is agreed.  
• Scotland-specific schemes (Mortgage to Rent/Shared Equity) are unique safeguards against repossession.  
• Records protect the borrower if the case later goes to court.

prompt: Are there any other schemes similar to Rent to Buy in the UK besides England?
response: In addition to Rent to Buy in England, there are different schemes in other parts of the UK. In Wales, the Rent to Own scheme has closed to new landlords, but some properties might still be available. In Scotland, there is no equivalent scheme, and in Northern Ireland, there is a different scheme. You should check with your local housing authority or relevant government websites to find out more about the schemes available in your area.
stratum: Housing and local services | procedural | medium | UK-wide | False | False
generated_response:
Rent to Buy is only run by Homes England, but the other UK nations have their own “rent now, buy later” offers.

1. Wales – Rent to Own – Wales  
   • You rent a newly built home from a participating housing association for 5 years.  
   • A share of the rent (and any increase in the home’s value) is set aside as a deposit.  
   • You then have the option to buy the home, using that deposit plus a normal mortgage.  
   Apply through a housing association listed on gov.wales or ask your local council’s housing team.

2. Northern Ireland – Rent to Own (Co-Ownership)  
   • You rent a new-build property from Co-Ownership for 3 years.  
   • Part of each monthly payment is saved toward a deposit.  
   • After 3 years you must buy the home outright, usually with a mortgage.  
   Start by registering with Co-Ownership (co-ownership.org).

3. Scotland – Mid-Market Rent-to-Buy pilots  
   • Some councils and housing associations offer “Rent to Buy” or “Rent Save Buy” on a pilot basis (for example, in the Highlands or by Link Housing).  
   • You rent for 5 years; part of the rent is put aside as a deposit; you then buy the property.  
   • Availability is limited and varies by area.  
   Check your local council’s housing pages or search “Rent to Buy Scotland” plus your council name.

Other affordable-ownership routes (all UK nations)  
• Shared Ownership / Co-Ownership  
• First Homes (England only)  
• Help to Buy (Scotland, Wales)  
See GOV.UK Affordable home ownership schemes: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services/owning-your-home

2. Visit the relevant national scheme website or speak to the local council’s housing advice team.  
3. Check eligibility (age, income, first-time buyer status).  
4. Register your interest and gather documents (ID, proof of income).  
5. View available properties and follow the application process set by the housing association or scheme operator.

prompt: I submitted a listed building consent application, but the decision came late. What should I do now?
response: If your listed building consent application decision was made after the 8-week deadline (or 13 weeks for major developments), you have the right to appeal. You must appeal within 6 months of the decision date or the date it was due. Only the person who applied can appeal, so ensure you are the correct applicant. Contact the Planning Inspectorate for detailed guidance on how to complete your appeal form and proceed with the process.

prompt: I'm trying to understand if I can get help with my mortgage interest payments. What are the conditions for receiving Support for Mortgage Interest (SMI)?
response: To receive Support for Mortgage Interest (SMI), you need to be getting a qualifying benefit. SMI helps with interest payments on your home's mortgage or loans for repairs and improvements. It's provided as a loan that you'll have to repay with interest when you sell or transfer ownership of your home. You can't use SMI for insurance policies or missed mortgage payments. You should check if you meet the criteria for a qualifying benefit and consider speaking to your lender for other possible options.
stratum: Housing and local services | procedural | medium | UK-wide | False | False
generated_response:
Support for Mortgage Interest (SMI) is a government-backed loan that can help pay the interest on your mortgage or certain home-improvement loans. To qualify, ALL the following conditions have to be met.

1. You or your partner must own the home (including shared-ownership and some houseboats or park homes).  
2. You must be receiving at least one of these income-related benefits:  
   • Universal Credit (UC) – you must have had no earned income for the last assessment period and have been on UC for 3 months in a row.  
   • Income Support – after receiving it for 39 weeks in a row.  
   • Income-based Jobseeker’s Allowance – after receiving it for 39 weeks in a row.  
   • Income-related Employment and Support Allowance – after receiving it for 39 weeks in a row.  
   • Pension Credit – no waiting period.  
   (You cannot get SMI if you are on contribution-based JSA/ESA only, Working Tax Credit, or most other benefits.)  

3. The loan you need help with must be:  
   • A mortgage or other qualifying loan taken out to buy your home, or  
   • A loan taken out for certain repairs or improvements (for example, fixing structural defects).  

4. The amount SMI can cover is limited to interest on up to £200,000 of outstanding mortgage (or up to £100,000 if you’re getting Pension Credit). It does not pay:  
   • The mortgage capital,  
   • Buildings insurance, service charges, or mortgage arrears.  

5. You must agree to take SMI as a LOAN:  
   • DWP pays the interest direct to your lender.  
   • A second-charge is placed on your property.  
   • You repay the SMI loan (with interest) when you sell or transfer ownership, or earlier if you choose.  

How to claim  
1. Contact the office that pays your qualifying benefit and say you want to claim Support for Mortgage Interest.  
2. They’ll send you an SMI loan offer letter and agreement form.  
3. Return the signed agreement within the deadline.  
4. DWP will arrange payments direct to your lender once the waiting period has passed.  

If your earnings start again while on Universal Credit, your SMI stops and the loan balance remains repayable later.  

More information and the current standard interest rate are on GOV.UK:  
https://www.gov.uk/support-for-mortgage-interest
extracted_justification:
• The key eligibility test for SMI is being on specific income-related benefits, so that is listed first and in full.  
• Waiting times and loan limits matter for planning, so they are stated clearly.  
• Explaining that SMI is now a repayable loan avoids later confusion.  
• Steps to claim are procedural and numbered for ease of use.  
• Provided the official GOV.UK link so the user can check the latest rules or rate.

prompt: I'm a landlord in England and my tenant has an excluded tenancy. What do I need to do if they refuse to leave after giving them reasonable notice?
response: If your tenant has an excluded tenancy, you don't need to go to court to evict them. You just need to give them 'reasonable notice' to quit, which is typically the length of the rental payment period. If they refuse to leave after this notice, you can change the locks on their rooms, even if they still have belongings in there. However, it's important to ensure that the notice period is appropriate and that you follow the correct procedures to avoid any potential legal issues.
stratum: Housing and local services | procedural | medium | UK-wide | False | False
generated_response:
If your tenant is an “excluded occupier” (for example a lodger who shares the kitchen or bathroom with you in your own home) you do not need a court order to evict them.  

1. Check they really are an excluded occupier  
   • They must live in your home and share living space or facilities with you.  
   • If they have self-contained accommodation (their own flat within the house) they are not excluded and you would need a court order.

prompt: I'm 17 and have been homeless for a few weeks. How does Children's Services help me find a place to live and what options are available if returning home isn't safe?
response: If you're 17 and homeless, Children's Services at your local council will usually provide you with accommodation as you're considered a 'child in need.' They'll explore options such as staying with a relative, foster care, or emergency housing. They cannot force you to return to a home where you feel unsafe. It's important to contact them promptly to access support and find a suitable living arrangement.

1. Get in touch straightaway

   • If you’re in danger right now, call 999 first.  

2. Ask for a “child in need” assessment  
   • Under the Social Services and Well-being (Wales) Act 2014 and the Children Act 1989, Children’s Services must assess your situation and needs.  
   • This should start the same day you contact them. You can ask to have a trusted adult or an independent advocate with you.  

3. Short-term (emergency) accommodation  
   • While they assess you, the council must give you somewhere safe to stay.  
   • They should avoid B&Bs; typical options are a foster placement, a supported lodgings host, or a staffed hostel with support workers.  

4. Longer-term options if home isn’t safe  
   • Become “looked after” under section 20 of the Children Act. You stay in foster care, a children’s home or semi-independent accommodation until you turn 18.  
   • Supported housing for 16–17-year-olds: your own room with on-site staff to help with budgeting, cooking, studying, etc.  
   • Staying-put or “when I’m ready” schemes: after 18 you may stay with your foster carer or in supported lodgings while you move into adulthood.  

5. Your rights while looked after  
   • A personal adviser and a pathway plan for education, training, work and housing up to age 21 (or 25 if in education).

6. Practical next steps today

   2. Keep any ID, letters or proof of homelessness with you.  
   3. Ring Shelter Cymru’s free helpline 08000 495 495 for independent advice.  
   4. If you feel pressured to return home, tell your social worker you believe it’s unsafe. They must take this into account.

prompt: If I get a Migration Notice, do I need to do anything else besides switching to Universal Credit?

prompt: I'm unsure whether I should appeal or complain about my tax credit decision. What's the difference?
response: Appealing and complaining are different processes for addressing issues with your tax credit decision. An appeal is used if you believe the decision was incorrect, such as an error in calculating your entitlement. A complaint is made if you feel you were treated unfairly or unprofessionally. You can start by contacting HMRC's helpline to discuss both options. They can guide you on which process is appropriate for your situation.
stratum: Benefits | procedural | medium | UK-wide | False | False
generated_response:
Appeal and complaint do two different jobs.

1. Appeal (called a “mandatory reconsideration” first, then an appeal):
   • Use this when you think HMRC’s decision about the amount you’re owed, an overpayment, or your entitlement is wrong.  
   • You ask HMRC to look at the decision again (mandatory reconsideration).  
   • If you still disagree, you can take the case to an independent tribunal.  
   • You must do this within 30 days of the decision letter.

2. Complaint:
   • Use this when the decision itself is correct but HMRC’s service was poor ­– for example delays, rude staff, lost papers or not following their own process.  
   • Complaining won’t change the decision, but you might get an apology, explanation or compensation.  
   • There is no strict time-limit, but complain as soon as possible.

Deciding what to do:

Step 1. Read your decision letter. Highlight the parts you think are wrong.  
Step 2. Ask yourself, “Do I want the amount or entitlement changed?”  
   – Yes → start a mandatory reconsideration (appeal route).  
   – No, I just want to complain about how I was treated → use the HMRC complaints process.  
Step 3. If both apply, you can run them side-by-side: submit the mandatory reconsideration for the decision and a separate complaint about service.

How to start:

Appeal route  
1. Fill in form TC846 or write to the address on your decision letter asking for a mandatory reconsideration.  
2. Keep copies and post by recorded delivery.  
3. Wait for the “mandatory reconsideration notice”.  
4. If still unhappy, submit form SSCS5 to the tribunal within 30 days of the notice.

Complaint route  
1. Phone HMRC Tax Credits helpline or write to the complaints address on GOV.UK.

prompt: If I'm self-employed and report zero income, will I still get Universal Credit?
response: Yes, you can still get Universal Credit if you report zero income, but your payment will be based on the minimum income floor, which is what an employed person on minimum wage would earn in similar circumstances. If you earn less than this, you may need to look for additional work to top up your income. Your payment is calculated based on your reported earnings each month.
stratum: Benefits | procedural | medium | UK-wide | False | False
generated_response:
Yes, you can still claim Universal Credit (UC) if you are self-employed and have a month where your business makes £0. How much you receive, or whether you receive anything at all, depends on whether the Minimum Income Floor (MIF) applies to you and on your wider household circumstances.

1. Check if the Minimum Income Floor applies  
   • If you have been self-employed for more than 12 months, UC usually assumes you earn a set amount each month (35 hours × National Living Wage for your age).  
   • If that MIF applies, UC is worked out as if you earned that amount—even when you report £0—so your UC payment could be reduced or reduced to £0.  
   • You can ask your work coach to suspend the MIF if you are still in a “start-up period”, have coronavirus-related problems, or exceptional circumstances.

2. Report your actual earnings and expenses each assessment period through your online UC account. Even if you earned nothing, you must still submit £0 so your claim stays live.

3. Make sure you meet the general UC rules  
   • Savings below £16,000 and household income (including any partner’s earnings) low enough once the MIF or actual earnings are taken into account.  
   • UK residency and other standard eligibility conditions.

4. If the MIF is switched off (for example, you are in your first 12 months of trading), UC will be calculated on your actual earnings—£0—so you are likely to receive a higher payment while your income is that low.

5. Next steps  
   1. Log in to your UC account each month and report £0 earnings if that is correct.  
   2. Ask your work coach whether the MIF is currently applied to your claim.  
   3. Use an independent benefits calculator or the GOV.UK “self-employed and Universal Credit” guidance to estimate your award.  
   4. If unsure, call the UC helpline or message your journal for a written decision.

• Benefits calculators – www.gov.uk/benefits-calculators

prompt: What happens if I move house while receiving Universal Credit?
response: If you change your address while receiving Universal Credit, the amount you get for housing each month will be based on your housing costs at the end of your assessment period. You need to report any changes in your circumstances to your work coach. This ensures that your Universal Credit payment reflects your current housing needs. Contact your local council or work coach for further assistance.

prompt: I think my tax credit decision is wrong, but I'm not sure how to proceed. What should I do?

   • Phone the Tax Credits Helpline on 0345 300 3900 (or 0345 300 3909 for textphone) and say you want a mandatory reconsideration, or

prompt: My business has debts and I want to close it. What are my options for dealing with the debts before closing?
response: If your business has debts, you have a few options to deal with them before closing. One option is to liquidate the company, which involves paying off debts and formally ending operations. Another option is to strike off the company from the Companies Register, which means it will no longer be active. However, you must ensure that all debts are settled or arranged for payment before proceeding. It's advisable to seek professional advice to navigate the legal and financial implications of each option.
stratum: Business and self-employed | procedural | medium | UK-wide | False | False
generated_response:
Here are the main ways small businesses in the UK deal with debts before they close. Which route is open to you depends largely on the legal structure of your business (sole trader, partnership or limited company) and whether you can still make at least some repayments.

1. Talk to your creditors first  
   • Explain that you intend to close the business and ask about reduced repayments, extra time or a one-off settlement.  
   • Get any new agreement in writing.

2. If you trade as a sole trader or partnership  
   a) Informal repayment plan – agree lower monthly payments with each creditor.  
   b) Debt Management Plan – made through a licensed debt-management provider; covers most unsecured debts.  
   c) Individual Voluntary Arrangement (IVA) – a formal, court-approved plan where you pay what you can (usually over 5 years); the rest is written off.  
   d) Bankruptcy – your assets are sold to pay creditors; remaining debts are written off. You can usually trade again after discharge (12 months) but your credit record is affected.

3. If you run a limited company  
   a) Company Voluntary Arrangement (CVA) – directors stay in control while an insolvency practitioner (IP) agrees a reduced repayment plan with creditors.  
   b) Creditors’ Voluntary Liquidation (CVL) – you choose to liquidate; an IP sells company assets and shares proceeds among creditors. Any remaining company debt is written off, but personal guarantees still stand.  
   c) Strike-off / dissolution – only possible if the company has no debts or the creditors all agree.  
   d) Administration – an IP takes control to rescue the business or get a better return for creditors; can lead to sale or liquidation.

4. Check for personal guarantees  
   • If you signed personal guarantees or traded while insolvent, you may be personally liable for some company debts. Get professional advice early.

5. Get professional help  
   • A licensed insolvency practitioner or a free debt-advice charity (e.g. Business Debtline 0800 197 6026) can explain the best route and handle paperwork.  
   • Their early involvement usually reduces costs and stress.

6. Tell HMRC and other authorities  
   • You must file final accounts and tax returns, cancel VAT/PAYE registrations and settle any remaining tax.  
   • If choosing bankruptcy, IVA, CVA or liquidation, the appointed practitioner will usually handle HMRC.

Relevant GOV.UK guidance  
Closing a limited company: https://www.gov.uk/closing-a-limited-company  
Company Voluntary Arrangement and administration: https://www.gov.uk/company-administration  
Bankruptcy: https://www.gov.uk/bankruptcy  
Individual Voluntary Arrangements: https://www.gov.uk/individual-voluntary-arrangements
extracted_justification:
• User wants to shut a debt-laden business; main missing fact is its legal form, so answer covers both scenarios.  
• Lists the recognised UK insolvency options in plain English, in the order a trader usually considers them.  
• Gives practical first actions (talk to creditors, seek advice) and highlights personal-guarantee risks.  
• Supplies authoritative GOV.UK links for deeper guidance and official procedures.

prompt: I'm part of a community group in Wales and we want to start an unincorporated association. Do we need to register it, and what happens if we make a profit?
response: No, you don't need to register an unincorporated association, and it doesn't cost anything to set one up. However, if the association starts making a profit through trading or other income sources, such as investments, you'll need to pay Corporation Tax and file a Company Tax Return, just like a limited company. This means you should keep accurate records of all financial activities and consult with a tax professional if needed.
stratum: Business and self-employed | procedural | medium | UK-wide | False | False
generated_response:
You do not have to register an unincorporated association in Wales (or anywhere in the UK). An unincorporated association is simply an agreement between a group of people to come together for a common, non-profit purpose.  

What to do:  
1. Agree a written constitution. This should cover:  
   • the group’s name and purpose  
   • how people become or leave being a member  
   • how decisions are made and meetings held  
   • how money is handled (bank account, two signatories, annual statement)  
   • what happens if the group closes  
2. Elect at least a chair, secretary and treasurer to act on the group’s behalf.  
3. Open a bank account in the association’s name (most banks will ask for the signed constitution and minutes showing who the officers are).  

If the group makes a surplus (profit):  
• Because an unincorporated association has no separate legal personality, the officers can be personally liable for its debts and any tax due.  
• Any surplus should be reinvested in the association’s activities.  
• If annual income goes over £1,000 (or at any level if you employ staff or own property) you may wish to register as a charity or another incorporated structure to limit personal liability.  
• If your yearly income exceeds £5,000 and you want charitable status you must register with the Charity Commission for England and Wales.  
• If you trade or provide paid-for services and your surplus after expenses is more than the annual Corporation Tax allowance (currently £50,000 for small non-corporate bodies) you must register with HMRC and file a tax return.  

Keeping the tax position safe:  
1. Keep clear records of all income and spending.  
2. Submit a Corporation Tax return if HMRC asks, or if you know tax is due.  
3. If you pay anyone, operate PAYE.  
4. Claim any available reliefs (for example, charitable exemption if you later register as a charity).  

Useful GOV.UK links  
• Setting up a business or community group: https://www.gov.uk/browse/business/setting-up  
• Corporation Tax for clubs and unincorporated associations: https://www.gov.uk/corporation-tax/non-resident-company-club-or-association  
• Charity registration requirements: https://www.gov.uk/running-charity/setting-up
extracted_justification:
• An unincorporated association is not required to register unless it seeks charitable status or another legal form.  
• A written constitution and elected officers give the group a clear operating framework and satisfy banks.  
• Surpluses belong to the members collectively; tax and personal liability arise because there is no separate legal entity.  
• Thresholds (£1,000–£5,000) trigger considerations of charity registration; Corporation Tax rules apply if trading profits arise.  
• GOV.UK links provide official guidance and contact points for detailed rules.

prompt: I'm a tenant in Scotland and I suspect there's asbestos in the building I rent. Who is responsible for managing it?
response: As a tenant in Scotland, your responsibility for managing asbestos depends on your lease or contract. If the lease specifies that the landlord is responsible, they must handle asbestos management. However, if the lease does not clarify this, you should check with your landlord or managing agent to determine who is accountable. If you suspect asbestos is present, you can request information from your landlord about its location and condition. Landlords are required to have an asbestos survey conducted by an accredited surveyor and to maintain records of findings. They must also ensure that asbestos-containing materials are kept in good repair or removed if necessary. If you believe your landlord is not fulfilling their responsibilities, you can seek advice from local authorities or housing organizations.

generated_response:

What you should do  
1. Tell your landlord or letting agent in writing why you think asbestos may be present (for example, crumbling insulation boards or old ceiling tiles).  
2. Ask them to confirm, in writing, what checks have been carried out and what they will do next.  
   • For common areas (stairwells, bin stores, lofts) they must, by law, have an asbestos survey and a management plan.  
   • Inside your flat they must meet the Repairing Standard, which means the property must be safe to live in. If asbestos-containing materials are damaged or likely to be disturbed, they must be repaired, sealed or removed by licensed contractors.  
3. Keep copies of all correspondence.  
4. If the landlord does not act within a reasonable time, contact your local council’s Environmental Health team for an inspection.  
5. As a last resort you can apply to the First-tier Tribunal for Scotland (Housing and Property Chamber) to enforce the Repairing Standard.  

Emergency situations  
• If asbestos-containing material is suddenly damaged (for example during DIY), leave the area, close the door, and call the landlord immediately.  
• Do not try to remove or dispose of asbestos yourself.  

Further information  
GOV.UK – Business and self-employed: Health and safety at work  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/business
extracted_justification:
• Under the Control of Asbestos Regulations 2012, the ‘duty-holder’ for non-domestic parts of a rented building is the landlord.  
• The Scottish Repairing Standard requires landlords to keep rented homes safe; damaged asbestos breaches this.  
• Tenants must notify the landlord first; local authority and tribunal routes exist if the landlord fails to act.  
• Tenants are not trained or licensed to handle asbestos, so the safest advice is to avoid disturbance and escalate appropriately.

prompt: How can I find out if a company has been wound up or is in the process of being liquidated?
response: To find out if a company has been wound up or is in the process of being liquidated, you can search The Gazette's website using the company's name or registration number. Winding up a company means it's being formally dissolved, often due to insolvency. Provisional liquidation occurs when a court freezes a company's assets before a final decision on liquidation is made. You should look for entries indicating insolvency or winding up. If the company is in this state, you may need to contact the relevant authorities or the appointed liquidator for more information.
stratum: Business and self-employed | procedural | medium | UK-wide | False | False
generated_response:
To see whether a company has been wound up or is already in liquidation, follow these steps.  
1. Go to the Companies House “Find and update company information” service (free to use).  
   • Type the company’s exact name or registration number.  
   • The company’s page will show its current “Company status”. If you see “Liquidation”, “In Administration”, “Voluntary Arrangement” or “Dissolved”, the business is being wound up or has already closed.  
2. Check the Insolvency Service’s “Individual and company insolvency register”.  
   • Search the company name. This confirms if an official insolvency practitioner has been appointed.  
3. Look at notices in The Gazette (the UK’s official public record).  
   • Choose “All notices” and enter the company name.  
   • Winding-up petitions, liquidation appointments and dissolution notices must be published here.  
4. Still unsure?  
   • Phone Companies House on 0303 123 4500 (Monday–Friday). Ask an adviser to read out the current status.  
   • If you prefer Welsh, select the Welsh-language option when the call connects.  
5. Keep a copy of anything you find (print-out or note). You may need it for proof in claims or disputes.  

Relevant GOV.UK starting page: https://www.gov.uk/browse/business
extracted_justification:

prompt: I recently sold some custom-made curtains online and a customer is returning them saying they are not as described. What should I do to handle this situation according to the law?
response: You must offer a refund if the item is faulty, not as described, or does not do what it's supposed to. Since the customer claims the curtains are not as described, you should check if they provided proof of purchase, like a receipt or packaging. If they did, you need to refund them in full. However, if they damaged the curtains or knew they were faulty when buying, you are not required to refund. You should also ensure that your return policy does not mislead customers, as it's illegal to display misleading notices. Always keep records of all communications and transactions.
stratum: Business and self-employed | procedural | medium | UK-wide | False | False
generated_response:
If you sell to a consumer in England you must follow the Consumer Rights Act 2015.  
When an item is “custom-made” the usual 14-day change-of-mind rule does not apply, but the buyer still has full rights if the goods are faulty or not as described.

What to do

1. Ask the buyer to explain exactly why the curtains are “not as described” and to send photos or other evidence.  
2. Compare their evidence with the wording and pictures in your listing, invoice and any messages.  
3. Decide:  
   a. If the curtains do differ from the description (size, colour, fabric, finish, etc.) they are “mis-described”.  
   b. If they match the description, you may reject the return but be prepared to justify this if the buyer escalates.  
4. Where they are mis-described:  
   • Offer the buyer a full refund within 14 days of receiving the returned curtains.  
   • Refund the original standard delivery charge.  
   • Arrange and pay for the return postage (or refund the buyer’s cost if they send them back).  
5. Keep written records of all messages, photographs, and the refund or refusal.  
6. If you and the buyer cannot agree, follow the dispute procedure of the selling platform or suggest free Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR).  
7. Review your listings to make sure future descriptions are clear and accurate.

Key GOV.UK guidance  
Accepting returns and giving refunds (for online sellers): https://www.gov.uk/accepting-returns-and-giving-refunds
extracted_justification:
• Under the Consumer Rights Act, goods must match the description; if not, the buyer is entitled to reject and get a refund.  
• Consumer Contracts Regulations remove the 14-day right to cancel for bespoke goods, but not the right to a remedy when mis-described.  
• Seller must bear return costs for faulty/mis-described goods.  
• Evidence and clear records protect the seller if the dispute goes to a platform claim, court or ADR.  
• GOV.UK link gives the official, up-to-date rules and further detail.

prompt: I need to check if another business's VAT number is valid. How do I go about doing this and what information will I get once I've checked it?
response: You can check a UK VAT number using the official government service provided. This will tell you if the number is valid and provide the business's name and address. To use this service, you'll need to enter the VAT number you want to check. If you're a VAT-registered business, you can also use this to prove when you checked the number, which might be needed for certain transactions. The service is available in both English and Welsh, making it accessible to a wider audience.
stratum: Business and self-employed | procedural | medium | UK-wide | False | False
generated_response:
You can check a UK-issued VAT number online and receive basic confirmation details.

1. Go to HMRC’s “Check a UK VAT number” service: https://www.gov.uk/check-uk-vat-number  
2. Enter the VAT number exactly as given (it will be 9 digits, or 12 digits for branches).  
3. Press ‘Check’.  
4. The service will tell you:  
   • whether the number is currently valid  
   • the date HMRC last confirmed its validity  
   • the registered business name and (if held) its principal place of business address  
5. Save or print the on-screen result for your records—HMRC accepts this as evidence that you took reasonable steps to verify the VAT number.  
6. If the service is unavailable, phone the VAT helpline (0300 200 3700, Monday–Friday) with the number and the business name you’ve been given.  
7. Checking a non-UK EU VAT number? Use the EU-wide VIES tool instead: https://ec.europa.eu/taxation_customs/vies/

What you will not see  
• No financial, turnover or contact information beyond the registered name and address.  
• Historic validity dates—only the current status and last checked date are shown.
extracted_justification:
